current_fact.fact_text: In 1970, China launched Dong Fang Hong I (“The East is Red 1”), its first satellite, becoming the fifth country to place an object into orbit with its own rocket. The satellite famously broadcast the song “The East Is Red” from space, turning an orbital beacon into a piece of musical propaganda.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

current_fact.match_reason: A satellite that literally played a song from orbit aligns with a cosmically tinged, groove‑driven set that feels like music beamed across borders and atmospheres.

queue.fact_text: On April 24, 1922, the first link of the Imperial Wireless Chain went live, sending wireless telegraph signals between Leafield in England and Cairo in Egypt. This network quietly shrank the British Empire by radio, stitching continents together with invisible pulses of Morse code.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: A story about early long‑distance signals and finite, deliberate transmissions pairs with an episode reflecting on intentional, non‑algorithmic listening and the value of each signal we send or receive.

queue.fact_text: In 1955, the Bandung Conference brought together 29 newly independent or non‑aligned Asian and African nations, who collectively condemned colonialism, racism, and great‑power domination. The meeting seeded the Non‑Aligned Movement and offered an alternate script for the Cold War world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A historic gathering against colonialism and domination resonates with an episode centered on Residente’s politically charged, anti‑imperialist storytelling.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1944, Britain’s Special Boat Service launched a covert raid on the German garrison occupying the Greek island of Santorini. The mission combined stealth, small boats, and the island’s volcanic geography in a little‑known chapter of Aegean World War II operations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A clandestine wartime raid on a Mediterranean island aligns with an episode themed around Mediterranean voyages, coasts, and sea stories.

queue.fact_text: In 1994, a vintage Douglas DC‑3 departing Sydney lost engine power and was forced to ditch into Botany Bay. Despite the dramatic water landing, all 25 people aboard survived, turning a potential tragedy into a rare example of a successful controlled ditching.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: An airplane surviving a water landing in a bay pairs with a sea‑voyage episode where water, resilience, and shared journeys are central motifs.

queue.fact_text: On April 24, 1990, Scotland’s Gruinard Island was officially declared free of anthrax after nearly half a century of quarantine following secret biological weapons tests during World War II. Decontamination involved soaking the island in seawater and formaldehyde, an eerie cleanup of a long‑hidden experiment.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: A strange Scottish island emerging from decades of contamination fits an episode grounded in Scottish landscapes, quiet transitions, and stories from the edges.

queue.fact_text: In 1980, Operation Eagle Claw—an ambitious U.S. mission to rescue hostages in Tehran—ended in disaster when a collision between a helicopter and a transport plane killed eight servicemen in the Iranian desert. The failed raid exposed the complexity of long‑range special operations and reshaped U.S. special forces.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: A politically charged military failure during the Iran hostage crisis pairs with an episode centered on Rage Against the Machine’s critiques of U.S. power and intervention.

queue.fact_text: On April 24, 1970, The Gambia shifted from monarchy to republic within the Commonwealth, with Dawda Jawara becoming its first president. This quiet constitutional change marked a deeper step in the country’s post‑colonial self‑definition.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A West African nation redefining its governance meshes with an episode celebrating African musical movements that paralleled political independence and new identities.

queue.fact_text: In 1924, Thorvald Stauning became Denmark’s prime minister, eventually shaping the foundations of the Danish welfare state and one of Europe’s earliest social democracies. His long tenure helped redefine how a modern state could blend capitalism, social safety nets, and labor rights.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: A leader balancing different social and economic forces connects with an episode about navigating contrasting energies—fog and fire—and harmonizing them through thoughtful storytelling.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 2011, WikiLeaks began releasing the Guantánamo Bay files, a trove of classified U.S. military documents detailing the detention and assessment of hundreds of prisoners. The leak offered a rare inside view of a tightly controlled wartime prison and reignited debates about secrecy, security, and human rights.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A massive digital leak challenging institutional secrecy pairs with an episode dissecting our digital lives, information overload, and how technology shapes what we know and share.

queue.fact_text: In 2004, the United States lifted 18‑year‑old economic sanctions on Libya after the country agreed to dismantle its weapons of mass destruction programs. The move turned a pariah state into a tentative partner, highlighting how science, weapons technology, and diplomacy intertwine.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: A North African state redefining its global role fits with a cross‑border musical journey that includes Maghreb sounds and themes of shifting destinations and identities.

queue.fact_text: On April 24, 1965, a civil war erupted in the Dominican Republic when Colonel Francisco Caamaño led a revolt that toppled the ruling triumvirate, aiming to restore the deposed president Juan Bosch. The conflict drew in U.S. troops and turned the Caribbean nation into a Cold War battleground over democracy and sovereignty.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

queue.match_reason: A Caribbean civil war entwined with U.S. power connects with an episode unpacking Latin urban music’s roots, politics, and the region’s contested histories.

queue.fact_text: In 1957, the Suez Canal reopened after being closed during the Suez Crisis, once United Nations peacekeepers were deployed to the region. The canal’s reopening restored one of the world’s most vital trade arteries and marked an early, dramatic test of UN peacekeeping.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A tense chokepoint reopening to global flow resonates with an episode about slowing down, reopening space, and letting currents—musical and emotional—move more freely again.

queue.fact_text: On April 24, 1916, the Easter Rising began as Irish republicans seized key locations in Dublin, proclaimed an Irish Republic, and held out for six days against British forces. Although initially unpopular and militarily doomed, the rebellion ignited a chain of events that led to Irish independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: An uprising that refused to ask permission for freedom pairs with an episode celebrating women who claimed rock’s loud, rebellious energy without waiting for approval.

queue.fact_text: In 1885, sharpshooter Annie Oakley joined Buffalo Bill’s Wild West show, turning her uncanny marksmanship into one of the era’s most famous traveling spectacles. She shattered expectations by outshooting male marksmen and became a symbol of frontier skill and a quietly subversive kind of female independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: A pioneering woman performer who defied gender norms fits a tribute episode to powerful female voices and unapologetic feminine presence.

queue.fact_text: According to the ancient Greek scholar Eratosthenes, April 24, 1183 BCE marks the traditional date of the Fall of Troy, the legendary end of the Trojan War. Whether myth or embellished memory, the story of a city taken by trickery and fire has echoed through literature, opera, and art for millennia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: A mythic siege retold for centuries aligns with an episode tracing another deep storytelling tradition—flamenco—where epic emotion, tragedy, and memory are sung rather than written.

queue.fact_text: The U.S. Embassy in Tehran was stormed in 1979, but on April 24, 1980, the failed rescue Operation Eagle Claw etched the Iran hostage crisis into military lore. The crisis itself, lasting 444 days, reshaped U.S.–Iran relations and became a defining drama of late‑Cold‑War diplomacy.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A geopolitical crisis that set the stage for decades to come fits with Sedna’s first raw, foundational episode—a kind of origin story amid tension and change.

queue.fact_text: On this day, Libya’s rapprochement with the West in 2004 hinged on dismantling clandestine weapons programs, including nuclear ambitions and chemical stockpiles. It was a rare modern example of a state voluntarily trading secret arsenals for sanctions relief and diplomatic thaw—though the country’s later history remained turbulent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A story about dismantling hidden, powerful technology parallels an episode about a studio tool that quietly transformed sound—and the debates about control versus expression that followed.

queue.fact_text: In the mid‑2000s, sanctions relief for Libya was framed as a reward for abandoning weapons programs, but it also underscored how economic isolation can reshape the fate of entire societies. The case became a cautionary example in debates over whether sanctions push regimes toward negotiation or harden them.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: An economic story about isolation and re‑opening to the world fits with an episode on travel, perspective shifts, and how movement—physical and mental—changes what’s possible.

queue.fact_text: While the exact date is legendary rather than scientific, the traditional Fall of Troy dated to April 24 around 1183 BCE reminds us how humans anchor myths to calendars. By pinning a war of gods and heroes to a specific day, ancient scholars blurred the line between history, astronomy, and storytelling.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A myth anchored by scholars like those of Alexandria pairs with an episode where people anchor their own stories to landscapes—mountains, seas, and the songs that turn memory into living myth.

published.fact_text: On April 24, 1990, the Space Shuttle Discovery released the Hubble Space Telescope into orbit, turning a school‑bus‑sized machine into humanity’s most famous observatory. Hubble’s images of distant galaxies, nebulae, and newborn stars fundamentally changed our understanding of the universe and its age.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A space telescope that turned cosmic emptiness into discovery fits an episode about reframing emptiness as space to create.

published.fact_text: In 1914, James Franck and Gustav Hertz presented an experiment showing that electrons lose energy in discrete jumps when colliding with mercury atoms. This quietly radical result became a cornerstone of quantum mechanics, proving that atoms have quantized energy levels.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A painstaking physics experiment that revealed quantized energy mirrors the episode’s theme of slow, effortful learning leading to breakthrough understanding.

published.fact_text: On April 24, 1916, Ernest Shackleton and five crewmen left the desolate Elephant Island in a small open lifeboat, the James Caird, to seek rescue 1,300 km away in South Georgia. Their improbable voyage through the stormy Southern Ocean became one of the most celebrated survival and navigation feats in polar history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: An Antarctic lifeboat riding ferocious seas pairs with a water‑themed episode about immersion, flow, and staying present in challenging conditions.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1967, Soviet cosmonaut Vladimir Komarov died when the parachute of his Soyuz 1 capsule failed to deploy during re-entry, making him the first human to die during an actual space mission. His flight exposed serious design flaws in the Soyuz program and became a stark reminder of the risks behind the space race.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A tragic mission that forced hard reflection on risk and progress pairs with a reflective episode about weathering storms and finding hope after dark chapters.

published.fact_text: In 1970, China launched Dong Fang Hong I (“The East is Red 1”), its first satellite, becoming the fifth country to place an object into orbit with its own rocket. The satellite famously broadcast the song “The East Is Red” from space, turning an orbital beacon into a piece of musical propaganda.
